critique.summary: The article introduces Edge.js, a JavaScript runtime designed to securely run Node.js workloads within a WebAssembly sandbox, aiming for high-density, fast startup, and compatibility with existing Node.js applications without containers. It highlights the architecture, performance, and advantages over traditional Node.js deployment methods.
mainPoints: Edge.js preserves full Node.js compatibility while isolating unsafe parts using WebAssembly and WASIX.
The architecture splits the sandbox into a JS engine and OS system calls, enabling native-like performance and security.
Edge.js aims to provide high-density, fast startup times, and secure execution for serverless and edge computing environments.
It supports running all Node.js modules and frameworks, including native modules, with high compatibility.
The project emphasizes performance improvements, with current speeds close to native Node.js and plans for further optimization.
critique.truthfulness: The article presents technical details about Edge.js, its architecture, and performance claims, which are consistent with known WebAssembly and Node.js compatibility principles. It references existing technologies like WASIX, NAPI, and compares with other runtimes such as Deno and Bun. However, as a promotional piece, it lacks independent third-party validation or detailed benchmarking data to fully verify performance claims. The claims about compatibility and security are plausible given the described architecture, but independent testing would be necessary for complete verification.
considerations: The article does not include independent benchmarks or third-party validation to substantiate performance and compatibility claims.
It primarily presents a promotional perspective, which may emphasize benefits while downplaying potential limitations or challenges.
Details about long-term stability, security guarantees, and real-world deployment experiences are limited.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the capabilities and potential use cases of Edge.js, a project that runs Node.js inside a WebAssembly sandbox with a pluggable JavaScript engine architecture. Commenters inquire about technical details, use cases, platform compatibility, and sandboxing possibilities, while the author provides clarifications.

comments.author: syrusakbary
comments.text:
Hi HN!

I'm Syrus, from Wasmer. We built Edge.js in a few weeks after different trials trying to bring Node.js to the Edge. We used AI and Codex heavily for this project, as otherwise the timeline would have spanned to a year plus to develop.

The summary of this announcement is that Edge.js:

  * Runs using WebAssembly when in `--safe` mode
  * It's fully compatible with Node.js (passing all their spec tests for non-VM modules)
  * It has a pluggable JS engine architecture: can work with V8, Javascript, SpiderMonkey, QuickJS, Hermes, etc.
Super happy to answer any questions you may have!
comments.analysis: As the creator, this comment provides authoritative and detailed information about Edge.js, its features, and development process, making it the most accurate.

comments.author: alex_reg
comments.text:
It's a bit confusing.

Roughly:

* a refactor of Node.js, but using a standardized API for JS engine interop * Integration with the Wasmer CLI so it will run JS with v8 but, everything else in Webassembly

Interesting idea.

Could be a much lighter weight way to sandbox JS...
comments.analysis: This comment offers a clear and informed summary of the technical approach behind Edge.js, showing understanding of the project's architecture.

comments.author: pacman1337
comments.text:
Not sure I understand the use cases. I'm guessing people want to run unsafe nodejs code. Either cloud providers like for lambdas or on personal computer for AI coding? On cloud why is this better than firecracker, firecrack can run any programming language this is just nodejs so already useless for that use case?

For personal computer, people worried about the spin up time of docker? I think that is more of a tooling issue where you spin up one instance and run multiple jobs.

What am I missing? What are actually real use cases where this would be better?
comments.analysis: Raises valid questions about practical applications and competitive advantages compared to existing solutions, contributing to the discussion.

comments.author: ammmir
comments.text: I don't get it. You mention being able to choose your own JS engine, so it's not using Wasmer's WebAssembly implementation but that of the chosen JS engine's? In other words, can Edge.js use Wasmer? Or have you managed to compile V8/JSC into WebAssembly and are executing it with Wasmer? If so, amazing!
comments.analysis: Seeks clarification on technical details regarding JS engine usage and WebAssembly implementation, showing engagement but some confusion.

comments.author: pscanf
comments.text:
Very cool project!

Question regarding the pluggable js engine: I have an electron app where I'm currently using QuickJS to run LLM-generated code. Would edge.js be able (theoretically) to use electron's v8 to get a "sanboxed within electron" execution environment?
comments.analysis: Poses a practical question about integration with Electron and sandboxing, reflecting real-world application interest.

comments.author: MillionOClock
comments.text: Very interesting! On what platforms can this run? If it can run on iOS, how would you handle attempts to access to the file system or networking, is this already wired in somehow? If not is it easy to add custom handlers to handle these actions?
comments.analysis: Asks relevant questions about platform support and security handling, indicating curiosity about implementation details.

comments.author: willquack
comments.text:
Awesome project!

Dumb question: could you run this in frontend js using the browser's js engine and wasm environment similar to WebContainers? Maybe `fs` is just in-memory, and some things like forking are disabled. It'd be cool to have "nodejs" in the web!
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and inquires about browser-based use cases, though the question is speculative and less technical.

comments.author: 2001zhaozhao
comments.text: Huh. Could this be a way to sandbox user-generated JS in web apps?
comments.analysis: Brief but relevant question about sandboxing user-generated code, showing interest in security applications.

comments.author: robjam
comments.text:
Wow! Node APIs in wasm(wasix)? Something that I have been thinking over for a long time is getting wasm (just wasm, not wasi or com) to be the unit of deployment/packaging for web apps like Nuxt/Next that only depend on, for example SQLite or Postgres. Slightly ignorant question, but would edge.js allow integrating with a caddy plug-in to handle serving the app from wasm?

I know of the extism project and played around with it in elixir, but I'm looking to have CF Workers DX with as little operational complexity as possible for many silly pet projects.
comments.analysis: Shares a conceptual idea related to wasm deployment and asks about integration possibilities, contributing a visionary perspective.

comments.author: actionfromafar
comments.text:
Can that Node app load and run WebAssembly?

(A.K.A. are we IBM 360 yet?)
comments.analysis: The comment is vague and uses an unclear analogy, adding little substantive value to the technical discussion.
